executiveSummary.summary: Strong team and differentiated approach to EV charging infrastructure, but faces significant capital intensity and utilization risks. The revenue-sharing model is clever, but unit economics are challenged by high upfront costs and uncertain rural demand. Success depends on careful site selection and potentially NEVI funding to improve economics.

marketSize.sam: $15-20B US fast charging market, with rural/suburban representing ~30-40%
marketSize.som: $50-100M realistic 5-year addressable market in target corridors
marketSize.methodology: Based on projected 30M EVs by 2030, 1 fast charger per 10-15 EVs in rural areas, $150K average station value

competitors.name: Electrify America
competitors.description: Walmart partnerships, highway focus, $2B Volkswagen investment
competitors.strength: Massive capital, established brand, prime locations
competitors.weakness: Slower rural expansion, limited revenue sharing

competitors.name: ChargePoint
competitors.strength: Largest US network, strong software platform
competitors.weakness: Mostly Level 2, limited fast charging in rural areas

competitors.name: EVgo
competitors.description: Fast charging network, grocery store partnerships

competitors.name: Tesla Supercharger
competitors.description: Opening to non-Tesla vehicles, highway corridors

marketAnalysis.positioning: The rural EV charging specialist with local business partnerships
marketAnalysis.timing: NEVI funding available now, rural EV adoption accelerating, infrastructure gap widening

valueProposition.coreProblem: EV owners in suburban/rural areas face range anxiety due to sparse fast charging infrastructure
valueProposition.targetCustomer: Rural/suburban EV owners and highway travelers passing through underserved corridors
valueProposition.uniqueAngle: Revenue-sharing partnerships with existing businesses instead of standalone stations
valueProposition.whyNow: EV adoption hitting rural areas, NEVI funding available, existing networks focused on urban markets

businessModel.revenueStrategy: Per-kWh charging fees plus subscription revenue, split with location partners
businessModel.pricingFramework: $0.35-0.45/kWh competitive with gas equivalent, $9.99/mo subscription for power users

unitEconomics.estimatedCAC: $50-100 per customer through app marketing and location visibility
unitEconomics.estimatedLTV: $800-1200 based on 2-3 year usage, 15-20 sessions/month for subscribers
unitEconomics.paybackPeriod: 4-7 years per station at 15-20% utilization, 3-4 years with NEVI co-funding

businessModel.scalabilityAssessment: Network effects improve with density, but capital requirements scale linearly with limited operational leverage

technicalFeasibility.recommendedStack: Cloud-native backend (AWS/Azure), React Native mobile app, OCPP-compliant charging hardware

buildVsBuy.rationale: ABB, ChargePoint hardware proven and certified
Core differentiator, partner revenue sharing unique

technicalFeasibility.estimatedMvpTimeline: 4-6 months for software platform, 6-12 months for first stations including permitting

risks.description: Low utilization rates make stations unprofitable - need 15-20% utilization to break even
risks.mitigation: Careful site selection, focus on highway corridors, NEVI funding to improve unit economics

risks.description: Tesla opening Superchargers to all EVs could dominate rural corridors
risks.mitigation: Move fast to secure prime locations, differentiate on local partnerships

risks.description: Rural EV adoption slower than projected, reducing demand growth
risks.mitigation: Focus on highway corridors with pass-through traffic, not just local demand

risks.description: NEVI program requirements complex, could delay funding or limit site options
risks.mitigation: Hire NEVI consultant, prepare dual track with private funding

risks.description: Grid connection costs and timeline unpredictable in rural areas
risks.mitigation: Partner with local utilities early, focus on locations with existing electrical capacity

week1to4: Analyze 50+ potential corridor sites with traffic data
Model unit economics for highway vs local scenarios
week5to8: Submit NEVI pre-applications for top 10 sites
week9to12: Prepare Series A materials assuming NEVI delays

decisionAgenda.question: Highway corridors vs local community charging focus

options.tradeoff: Higher utilization but more competition vs lower utilization but less competition
Less competition but uncertain demand vs proven demand but competitive

decisionAgenda.recommendation: Highway corridors - utilization rates are make-or-break, and highway traffic is more predictable than local EV adoption

decisionAgenda.question: NEVI funding vs private capital speed

options.tradeoff: Better unit economics but 6-12 month delay vs faster market entry but worse economics
Speed to market but capital efficiency risk vs funding certainty but timing risk

decisionAgenda.recommendation: Dual track - apply for NEVI while building with private capital. Economics may not work without co-funding.

decisionAgenda.question: Geographic expansion strategy

options.tradeoff: Operational efficiency vs limited market size

decisionAgenda.recommendation: Start with 2-3 corridors in adjacent states to prove model before broader expansion
